input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Digital Twin system** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive manufacturing systems** (Documents [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17]). The **physical twin** is characterized as follows:

1. **Type of System**:
   - **Cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]): Hybrid systems integrating **biological, physical, and cyber components** (e.g., human-machine interactions, biological sensors, or embedded systems with real-time feedback loops).
   - **Automotive manufacturing systems** (Documents [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17]): **Industrial IoT (IIoT)-enabled manufacturing lines**, including **vehicle assembly, battery systems (e.g., electric vehicle (EV) batteries)**, and **automotive production lines** with **real-time monitoring and adaptive control**.

2. **Main Components**:
   - **Physical Layer**: Sensors (e.g., IoT sensors for temperature, vibration, or chemical composition), actuators, and **physical assets** (e.g., machinery, vehicles, or battery cells).
   - **Cyber Layer**: **Embedded systems**, **edge devices**, and **cloud/edge computing platforms** for data processing (Document [14]).
   - **Biophysical Layer** (Document [7]): **Biological interfaces** (e.g., wearable sensors, biological markers) integrated with physical systems for **closed-loop cyber-biophysical control**.
   - **Automotive-Specific Components** (Documents [8], [9], [17]): **Battery management systems (BMS)**, **charging infrastructure**, and **vehicle-to-grid (V2G) interfaces**.

3. **Operational Domain**:
   - **Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments** (Document [12]) with **real-time data acquisition** and **adaptive manufacturing processes**.
   - **Automotive domain**: **Mass production lines** (Document [8]), **EV battery systems** (Document [17]), and **on-orbit aerospace applications** (Document [16], though not automotive-specific).
   - **Cyber-biophysical domain**: **Human-in-the-loop systems** (Document [7]), where biological signals (e.g., EEG, ECG) are used for **adaptive control** of physical processes.

4. **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
   - **Dynamic and adaptive**: Systems require **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital models (Document [10]).
   - **Multi-domain integration**: Combines **physical, cyber, and biological layers** (Document [7]) with **IIoT protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) for data exchange (Document [12]).
   - **High fidelity**: Demands **accurate simulation models** (e.g., finite element analysis (FEA), multi-physics simulations) for **predictive maintenance** and **optimization** (Document [15]).
   - **Scalability**: Supports **distributed architectures** (e.g., edge computing for local processing, cloud for global analytics) (Document [14]).
   - **Standardized frameworks**: Adheres to **ISO 23247** (Documents [10], [14], [16], [17]) for **digital twin architecture**, ensuring interoperability across domains.
   - **Biophysical feedback loops**: Enables **closed-loop control** where biological data (e.g., stress levels, fatigue) influences physical system behavior (Document [7]).
   - **Automotive-specific traits**: **Battery degradation modeling**, **thermal management**, and **energy efficiency optimization** (Documents [9], [17]).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin system** provides the following **specific services**, categorized by type, target users, and capabilities, as detailed in the documents:

1. **Monitoring Services**:
   - **Real-time telemetry**: Continuous collection and analysis of **sensor data** (e.g., vibration, temperature, battery health) from physical systems (Documents [8], [9], [10], [12]).
   - **Condition monitoring**: **Predictive maintenance** using **machine learning (ML)** and **time-series forecasting** to detect anomalies (Document [15]).
   - **Biophysical monitoring** (Document [7]): Integration of **biological sensor data** (e.g., EEG, ECG) to assess **operator stress or fatigue** in human-machine interactions.
   - **Energy monitoring**: Real-time tracking of **battery state of charge (SoC)**, **state of health (SoH)**, and **thermal profiles** in automotive systems (Document [17]).

2. **Optimization Services**:
   - **Process optimization**: **Adaptive control** of manufacturing processes (e.g., **automotive assembly lines**) using **reinforcement learning (RL)** or **model predictive control (MPC)** (Document [14]).
   - **Resource allocation**: Optimization of **energy distribution** in **V2G systems** or **battery charging schedules** (Document [9]).
   - **Thermal management optimization**: Adjusting **cooling systems** in automotive batteries to extend lifespan (Document [17]).
   - **Biophysical optimization** (Document [7]): Dynamic adjustment of **human workload** or **ergonomic parameters** based on real-time biological feedback.

3. **Prediction Services**:
   - **Failure prediction**: **ML-based forecasting** of equipment failures (e.g., **bearing wear**, **battery degradation**) using **digital twin simulations** (Document [15]).
   - **Performance prediction**: Simulating **lifecycle behavior** of automotive components (e.g., **battery cells**) under varying operational conditions (Document [9]).
   - **Biophysical prediction**: Forecasting **operator performance decline** or **fatigue-related errors** in cyber-biophysical systems (Document [7]).
   - **On-orbit collision avoidance** (Document [16]): Predictive modeling for **space debris detection** using digital twin simulations.

4. **Control Services**:
   - **Closed-loop control**: **Real-time actuation** of physical systems based on digital twin outputs (e.g., **adjusting motor speeds** in manufacturing) (Document [10]).
   - **Autonomous decision-making**: **AI-driven control** for **autonomous vehicles** or **robotic assembly systems** (Document [15]).
   - **Biophysical control** (Document [7]): **Adaptive interfaces** where digital twin models adjust **human-machine interactions** (e.g., **ergonomic feedback**).
   - **Energy control**: **Dynamic load balancing** in **V2G systems** or **battery management** (Document [9]).

5. **Visualization Services**:
   - **3D/4D digital twin visualization**: **Immersive simulations** (e.g., **VR/AR**) for **manufacturing planning**, **troubleshooting**, and **operator training** (Document [10]).
   - **Real-time dashboards**: **IoT-enabled dashboards** for **automotive engineers** to monitor **battery health**, **production metrics**, or **biophysical parameters** (Document [8]).
   - **Predictive visualization**: **What-if scenario simulations** (e.g., **battery aging under extreme temperatures**) (Document [17]).

6. **Target Users**:
   - **Engineers**: For **design validation**, **process optimization**, and **troubleshooting** (Documents [8], [10], [12]).
   - **Manufacturers**: For **predictive maintenance** and **quality control** in **automotive production lines** (Document [9]).
   - **Operators**: For **biophysical feedback** (e.g., **ergonomic alerts**) in **human-machine systems** (Document [7]).
   - **Automotive OEMs**: For **battery lifecycle management** and **energy efficiency** (Document [17]).
   - **Aerospace/Astronautics**: For **on-orbit collision avoidance** and **space debris detection** (Document [16]).

7. **Service Capabilities**:
   - **Cross-domain interoperability**: Integration of **physical, cyber, and biological data** via **standardized APIs** (e.g., **OPC UA**, **MQTT**) (Document [12]).
   - **Edge-cloud hybrid processing**: **Low-latency decision-making** at the edge while leveraging **cloud analytics** for global insights (Document [14]).
   - **AI/ML integration**: **Neural networks** for **anomaly detection**, **generative models** for **digital twin reconstruction**, and **RL for control** (Document [15]).
   - **Standard compliance**: Alignment with **ISO 23247** for **digital twin architecture**, ensuring **reproducibility** and **scalability** (Documents [10], [14], [16], [17]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin system** leverages the following **tools, technologies, frameworks, and enablers**, as explicitly mentioned in the documents:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247 Compliance Framework** (Documents [10], [14], [16], [17]): The **foundational standard** for digital twin architecture, defining **layers** (e.g., **physical, cyber, and service layers**), **data models**, and **interoperability protocols**.
   - **Edge Computing Frameworks** (Document [14]): **Edge-based digital twin frameworks** (e.g., **Kubernetes**, **Docker**) for **low-latency processing** of **IIoT sensor data** in **automotive manufacturing**.
   - **AI/ML Platforms** (Document [15]): **TensorFlow**, **PyTorch**, and **scikit-learn** for **predictive modeling**, **anomaly detection**, and **reinforcement learning** in digital twin simulations.
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Multi-physics simulation tools** (e.g., **ANSYS**, **COMSOL**) for **thermal**, **structural**, and **electrical modeling** of **automotive components** (Document [9]).
     - **Digital twin simulation platforms** (e.g., **Siemens Digital Twin**, **PTC ThingWorx**) for **real-time co-simulation** (Document [10]).
     - **Battery simulation tools** (e.g., **BatterySim**, **PSAT**) for **lifecycle modeling** of **EV batteries** (Document [17]).

2. **Development Tools**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools** (Document [7]): **SysML**, **UML**, and **Modelica** for **digital twin modeling** and **system specification**.
   - **Digital Twin Modeling Languages**: **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document [11]) for **ontology-based modeling** of **cyber-biophysical systems**.
   - **Low-Code/No-Code Platforms**: **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**, **AWS IoT TwinMaker** for **rapid prototyping** of digital twin applications (Document [14]).
   - **Version Control and Collaboration**: **Git**, **Jira**, and **Confluence** for **digital twin development lifecycle management** (Document [12]).

3. **Databases and Data Management**:
   - **Time-Series Databases**: **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB** for **high-frequency sensor data** (e.g., **battery telemetry**, **vibration monitoring**) (Document [8]).
   - **Graph Databases**: **Neo4j**, **ArangoDB** for **relationship modeling** of **cyber-biophysical dependencies** (Document [7]).
   - **Digital Twin Data Models**: **JSON-LD**, **RDF** for **semantic interoperability** (Document [11]).
   - **Blockchain for Data Integrity**: **Hyperledger Fabric** for **secure, immutable logging** of **digital twin transactions** (Document [12]).

4. **Simulation and AI Technologies**:
   - **Generative AI**: **Generative adversarial networks (GANs)** for **digital twin reconstruction** from sparse data (Document [15]).
   - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)**, **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** for **adaptive control** in **automotive manufacturing** (Document [14]).
   - **Digital Twin Reconstruction**: **Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)** for **high-fidelity digital twin generation** (Document [15]).
   - **Explainable AI (XAI)**: **SHAP**, **LIME** for **interpreting digital twin predictions** (Document [15]).

5. **Communication Protocols and Standards**:
   - **Industrial IoT Protocols**: **OPC UA**, **MQTT**, **AMQP** for **real-time data exchange** between **physical systems** and **digital twins** (Document [12]).
   - **5G/6G Networks**: **Ultra-low latency** communication for **edge-based digital twin control** (Document [14]).
   - **Semantic Web Standards**: **OWL**, **RDFS** for **ontology-based digital twin modeling** (Document [11]).

6. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **Biophysical Interfaces** (Document [7]): **Wearable sensors** (e.g., **EEG headsets**, **EMG gloves**) for **human-machine interaction modeling**.
   - **Digital Twin for Battery Systems** (Document [17]): **Thermal management models**, **aging prediction algorithms**, and **charging optimization algorithms**.
   - **On-Orbit Digital Twin** (Document [16]): **Space debris tracking algorithms**, **collision avoidance simulations**, and **AI-driven trajectory optimization**.
   - **Systems of Twinned Systems** (Document [13]): **Meta-digital twin architectures** for **orchestrating multiple digital twins** (e.g., **automotive supply chain**, **human-machine ecosystems**).

7. **Hardware Enablers**:
   - **Edge Devices**: **Raspberry Pi**, **NVIDIA Jetson** for **local digital twin processing** (Document [14]).
   - **FPGA/ASIC Acceleration**: **Hardware-accelerated simulations** for **real-time digital twin control** (Document [12]).
   - **Quantum Computing**: **Potential future integration** for **ultra-fast digital twin optimization** (Document [15]).

8. **Standardization and Compliance**:
   - **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document [11]): **Ontology framework** for **digital twin concepts and terminology**.
   - **ISO 23247** (Documents [10], [14], [16], [17]): **Digital twin framework** for **manufacturing**, **aerospace**, and **automotive applications**.
   - **IEEE P2418** (implied in [12]): **Digital twin taxonomy** for **IIoT systems**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application standards despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by real-time operational requirements, with ROS2-based function execution (implemented as individual packages) ensuring low-latency synchronization across entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity). The system’s time scale is aligned with automotive safety-critical constraints, where latency in data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication (hosted in the Physical Twin Entity) must comply with the 100 ms threshold to maintain coherence between the physical and digital domains. No explicit mention of service-specific time scales or hierarchical temporal granularity is provided beyond this synchronization requirement.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary multiplicities: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247’s *Operation Management Entity (OME)* by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) within the Physical Twin Entity. The *User Interface Function Element (FE)* is further split into two subcomponents (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*), while the *Data Storage* component lacks a direct ISO 23247-aligned FE. Coordination between entities relies on ROS2 packages (each containing one or more ROS2 nodes) and their inter-node communication, with no explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances or centralized orchestration mechanisms beyond the described entity decomposition.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)-inspired orchestration pattern**, where software components are mapped to *Function Elements (FEs)* and deployed as ROS2 packages on Field Elements (FEs). The architecture emphasizes **entity-based modularity**, with the *Digital Twin Entity* managing data management and control mechanisms, the *Physical Twin Entity* handling resource-specific operations (e.g., sensors, actuators, and compute), and the *User Entity* housing user-facing interfaces (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*). Resource management is distributed across entities, and system-wide coordination is achieved through ROS2’s inter-node communication framework, enabling real-time synchronization and data exchange. The design introduces deviations from ISO 23247 by merging functionalities (e.g., general automotive tasks in the Physical Twin Entity) and omitting certain FEs (e.g., *Data Storage*), which may impact ownership and engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** reference architecture, which defines an **entity-based framework** where the **Digital Twin Entity** represents the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) through computational models and data components. The system integrates **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** to simulate OMEs (e.g., equipment, facilities, processes) with high fidelity. Data sources include **real-time sensor feeds** and **historical manufacturing data**, managed via standardized protocols such as **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability. The **Digital Twin Entity** interfaces with **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which may implement specific tasks like data transformation, human-interpretable visualization, or OME actuation. Model relationships are established through **entity decomposition**, where each FE contributes to the twin’s overall functionality, though the standard does not prescribe implementation specifics beyond modularity. Data management approaches rely on **entity-based separation of concerns**, ensuring modularity and scalability in the twin’s information exchange framework (ISO 23247 Part 4).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The **physical acting components** in this Digital Twin system are governed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** (as per ISO 23247). This entity is responsible for **remote control mechanisms** and **actuation capabilities** over Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs). While the standard does not specify exact actuator types or ranges, it implies support for **OPC UA-compatible control interfaces** for bidirectional communication between the Digital Twin and physical systems. Safety constraints are inferred to be managed via **entity-level protocols**, though explicit details on actuation ranges, safety mechanisms, or addressing schemes (e.g., IP-based or device-specific) are **not provided in the documents**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **physical sensing infrastructure** is defined within the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which collects data from OMEs (e.g., equipment, processes). The standard does not detail **specific sensor types, spatial distribution, or sampling frequencies**, but it implies integration with **real-time data feeds** for observable manufacturing elements. Measurement capabilities are assumed to align with OPC UA’s **standardized data models**, enabling high-resolution data transmission. However, **accuracy/precision specifications, transmission rates, or data collection mechanisms** (e.g., edge vs. cloud processing) are **not explicitly mentioned in the documents**.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **fidelity and validity** of the Digital Twin system are implicitly addressed through the **ISO 23247 reference architecture**, which mandates **entity-based validation** for Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs). While the standard does not prescribe **quantitative methods for uncertainty quantification** or **error detection/correction mechanisms**, it emphasizes **model verification via functional entity (FE) implementation** (e.g., data transformation, user interfaces). Quality assurance processes are inferred to rely on **standardized information exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA) to ensure consistency between physical and digital representations. However, **specific validation methods, uncertainty sources, or fidelity requirements** (e.g., temporal/spatial resolution) are **not detailed in the provided documents**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors) and their transformation into digital representations. **Data types transmitted** include raw point cloud data (2D/3D), LiDAR clustering/tracking outputs, vehicle state metrics (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo angles), and object tracks. **Preprocessing steps** are executed by the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**, which clusters and tracks point cloud data to derive meaningful object representations. **Communication protocols** for data transmission are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **ROS2** for intra-entity communication and **UDP over Wi-Fi** for inter-entity data propagation. **Transmission frequencies** are implied to be high (real-time), as the system displays dynamic vehicle state and projected driving paths (e.g., ACC-relevant data). **Data quality assurance** is inferred through the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component, which filters and structures raw LiDAR data into actionable digital representations. **Data integration mechanisms** involve the **Data Translation FE**, which serializes, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for all data streams to ensure compatibility across the system. **Event triggers** include sensor activations (e.g., LiDAR scans) and vehicle state changes, which are processed and visualized in real-time via the **3D Visualization FE** (e.g., Fig. 3a).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated through **control signals** generated by the **Controlling FE**, which interfaces with the **Actuation FE** to execute commands on physical actuators (motor, steering servo). **Command types** include **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) inputs**, which are translated via the **Data Translation FE** to ensure proper serialization and unit alignment. **Control message formats** are standardized through ROS2 communication, with **UDP-based Wi-Fi** enabling inter-entity command transmission. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on **Data Translation FE** for message integrity (serialization/unit conversion). **Feedback systems** are implied through real-time **vehicle state monitoring** (e.g., motor/steering servo responses), which are visualized in the **3D Visualization FE** for operator confirmation. **Conflict resolution** is not mentioned, but the system’s reliance on **ACC inputs** suggests hierarchical control prioritization. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred via the **Reporting FE**, which generates reports on system performance and data storage, though explicit feedback loops (e.g., error signals) are not described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** is built on a **multi-layered network architecture** combining **ROS2** for intra-entity communication (software components within the Digital Twin Entity) and **UDP over Wi-Fi** for inter-entity communication (between Physical and Digital Twin Entities). **Communication protocols** include:
- **ROS2** (intra-entity, topic-based messaging for SW components).
- **UDP** (inter-entity, transport-layer protocol for low-latency data transmission).
**Network topology** is not explicitly detailed, but the system implies a **distributed architecture** with sensor data flowing from Physical Twin → Data Pre-Processing FE → Digital Twin Entity → Controlling FE → Physical Actuation. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are critical for real-time applications (e.g., ACC), though specific metrics (e.g., Mbps, ms) are not provided. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not mentioned, but the **Data Translation FE** ensures data consistency through serialization and unit conversion. **Security protocols** are not described, though UDP/Wi-Fi suggests potential vulnerabilities (e.g., lack of encryption) unless mitigated by external systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Key inferred aspects include:
- **Hosting platforms**: Not specified (could be **edge computing** given real-time sensor processing or **cloud-based** for storage/reporting).
- **Computational requirements**: Implied by **Data Pre-Processing FE** (point cloud clustering/tracking) and **Controlling FE** (ACC logic), suggesting moderate-to-high CPU/GPU needs for real-time processing.
- **Storage requirements**: **Data Storage FE** and **Reporting FE** indicate persistent storage for raw/processed data and generated reports, but specifics (e.g., TB capacity) are absent.
- **Scalability mechanisms**: Not described, though ROS2’s modularity and UDP’s statelessness suggest potential for horizontal scaling.
- **Deployment models**: Likely **hybrid** (edge for sensor processing, cloud for analytics/reporting), but not confirmed.
- **Availability/reliability assurance**: Not detailed, though **Data Translation FE** ensures data integrity across components. **Redundancy mechanisms** (e.g., failover) are not mentioned.
**Not in Document** for precise details on cloud/edge/on-premise deployment, exact hardware specs, or fault-tolerance strategies.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as outlined in **ISO 23247 Part 2**, follows an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where the lifecycle is structured around four primary **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—equipment, facilities, products, personnel, and processes—each mapped to corresponding **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. The lifecycle stages include:

1. **Conceptualization & Design Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: Abstracted **Functional Entities (FEs)** are defined to modularize DT functionality (e.g., data collection, user interaction, or OME control). These FEs are not standardized in implementation but may leverage protocols like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability.
   - **Transition Management**: The OME is identified as the physical counterpart to the DT, with its observable attributes (e.g., operational states, sensor data) serving as the foundational input for the DTE. The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** bridges the physical and digital domains by capturing real-time or historical data.

2. **Development & Deployment Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: The DTE is instantiated as a **digital representation** of the OME, incorporating **real-time data streams** (e.g., from IoT sensors) and **historical data** for simulation or analysis. The DTE may include **virtual models** (e.g., CAD, physics-based simulations) aligned with the OME’s physical behavior.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: The **User Entity** provides interfaces (e.g., dashboards, APIs) for monitoring or controlling the OME via the DTE. Governance is implied through the **entity-based RA**, where each entity’s responsibilities (e.g., data validation, security) are explicitly defined but not detailed in the standard.

3. **Operation & Maintenance Phase**:
   - **Representation Type**: The DT evolves dynamically with **real-time updates** from the OME, enabling **closed-loop control** (e.g., predictive maintenance, process optimization). The DTE may incorporate **machine learning models** (not specified in the standard) to adapt to operational changes.
   - **Transition Management**: Data exchange between entities is governed by **information exchange standards** (referenced in ISO 23247 Part 4), though specifics (e.g., protocols, formats) are not detailed. The lifecycle assumes iterative updates to the DTE based on OME performance or environmental changes.

4. **Retirement & Legacy Management Phase**:
   - **Not explicitly addressed** in the provided documents. The standard focuses on the **active lifecycle** of DTs in manufacturing, with no guidance on archival, decommissioning, or legacy system integration.

**Key Governance Approaches**:
- **Modularity**: FEs enable **plug-and-play** integration of new functionalities (e.g., adding a new analytics module) without redesigning the entire DT.
- **Interoperability**: While not detailed, the standard implies adherence to **industry-wide protocols** (e.g., OPC UA) for seamless data exchange between entities.
- **Traceability**: The entity-based RA ensures **end-to-end traceability** from physical OMEs to digital representations, facilitating audits and compliance.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** in the context of **ISO 23247** are governed by an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** with the following methodologies and practices:

1. **Engineering and Development Process**:
   - **Methodology**: The DT is developed using a **modular, entity-centric approach**, where each **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** (e.g., equipment, processes) is paired with a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**. The DTE is constructed by assembling **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which are smaller, specialized components responsible for tasks such as:
     - **Data Collection**: Interfacing with sensors or IoT devices via **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** or other standardized protocols.
     - **Data Transformation**: Converting raw data into **human-interpretable formats** (e.g., dashboards, reports) or **machine-readable models** (e.g., physics-based simulations).
     - **User Interaction**: Providing interfaces (e.g., HMI, APIs) for monitoring or controlling the OME.
     - **Control Actuation**: Executing commands on the physical OME (e.g., adjusting parameters in real-time).
   - The standard does **not prescribe** specific implementation technologies for FEs, allowing flexibility (e.g., custom scripts, commercial software, or open-source tools).

2. **Quality Assurance (QA) Practices**:
   - **Not explicitly detailed** in the provided documents. However, the **entity-based RA** implies that QA is distributed across entities, with each FE responsible for ensuring:
     - **Data Accuracy**: Validation of inputs from the OME (e.g., sensor calibration, signal processing).
     - **Functional Correctness**: Testing of FE outputs (e.g., simulation fidelity, control logic).
   - The standard references **information exchange standards** (ISO 23247 Part 4) for ensuring data integrity during transitions between entities.

3. **Testing and Validation Approaches**:
   - **Validation**: The DTE is validated against the **physical OME** through **real-time comparison** of operational metrics (e.g., performance indicators, energy consumption). The standard does not specify validation frameworks but implies iterative testing during the **development phase**.
   - **Simulation-Based Validation**: The DTE may incorporate **virtual models** (e.g., CAD, finite element analysis) to validate behavior before deployment. The standard does not detail validation criteria or tools.

4. **Version Control**:
   - **Not explicitly addressed**. The standard focuses on the **functional decomposition** of the DT rather than versioning strategies. However, the **modular FE design** suggests that version control could be applied at the FE level (e.g., using Git for code-based FEs or configuration management for software-based FEs).

5. **Evolution Management Strategies**:
   - **Dynamic Updates**: The DT evolves through **real-time data feeds** from the OME, enabling continuous adaptation to operational changes (e.g., wear-and-tear, process adjustments). The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** ensures that updates propagate to the DTE.
   - **FE Upgrades**: New or updated FEs can be integrated without redesigning the entire DT, leveraging the **modular architecture**. For example:
     - Adding a **predictive maintenance FE** to analyze sensor data.
     - Replacing an outdated **data transformation FE** with a machine learning-based version.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: The **User Entity** may provide tools for **manual intervention** (e.g., parameter tuning, FE configuration) to align the DT with evolving OME requirements.

**Key Evolution Mechanisms**:
- **Closed-Loop Feedback**: The DT and OME interact bidirectionally, with the DTE adjusting its behavior based on real-time OME performance.
- **Adaptive Modeling**: While not specified, the standard implies that the DTE can incorporate **updated virtual models** (e.g., revised CAD data) to reflect changes in the OME’s design or environment.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **decision support capabilities** of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as implied by **ISO 23247**, are centered around **real-time analytics, user interaction, and confidence-driven recommendations**, though specific technical details are limited to the **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. Key components include:

1. **Types of Insights Generated**:
   - **Operational Monitoring**: The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** processes data from the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** to generate **real-time insights** on:
     - **Performance Metrics**: Key performance indicators (KPIs) such as throughput, efficiency, or energy consumption, derived from **sensor data** collected by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**.
     - **Anomaly Detection**: Identification of deviations from expected operational patterns (e.g., equipment malfunctions) via **comparative analysis** against historical or simulated data.
     - **Predictive Indicators**: Early warnings for potential failures or inefficiencies, though the standard does not specify **predictive algorithms** (e.g., machine learning models) or time horizons (e.g., short-term vs. long-term forecasting).
   - **Process Optimization**: Insights into **process bottlenecks** or **resource allocation inefficiencies**, enabled by **data transformation Functional Entities (FEs)** that aggregate and analyze OME-specific metrics.

2. **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
   - **Data Processing Pipeline**: The DTE integrates **real-time and historical data** through a pipeline governed by the **entity-based RA**:
     - **Raw Data Collection**: Sensor data is ingested via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, often using **OPC UA** or similar protocols.
     - **Data Enrichment**: FEs transform raw data into **structured formats** (e.g., time-series databases, feature vectors) for analysis.
     - **Analytical Processing**: While the standard does not detail specific analytics (e.g., statistical methods, AI/ML), it implies support for:
       - **Descriptive Analytics**: Summarizing OME performance (e.g., dashboards, reports).
       - **Diagnostic Analytics**: Root-cause analysis of operational issues (e.g., fault tree analysis).
       - **Prescriptive Analytics**: Generating **actionable recommendations** (e.g., maintenance schedules, process adjustments), though confidence measures are not specified.
   - **Simulation and Virtualization**: The DTE may incorporate **virtual models** (e.g., physics-based simulations) to validate insights against **what-if scenarios** (e.g., testing process changes before deployment).

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: Decisions are executed through **bidirectional interaction** between the DTE and OME:
     - **Monitoring Mode**: The **User Entity** reviews insights (e.g., via dashboards) and manually intervenes (e.g., adjusting parameters).
     - **Autonomous Mode**: The DTE may **automatically actuate** the OME based on predefined rules or **real-time analytics** (e.g., triggering maintenance alerts). The standard does not specify **control algorithms** (e.g., PID, reinforcement learning).
   - **Human-in-the-Loop**: The **User Entity** serves as the primary interface for **decision validation**, ensuring alignment between DT insights and operational goals.

4. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Interfaces**: The **User Entity** provides:
     - **Visualization Tools**: Dashboards or 3D models for **real-time monitoring** of OME performance.
     - **Command Interfaces**: APIs or HMIs for **manual control** of the OME via the DTE.
     - **Alerting Systems**: Notifications for critical events (e.g., equipment failures) based on **anomaly detection** in the DTE.
   - **Access Control**: While not detailed, the standard implies **role-based access** to ensure authorized users can interact with the DT (e.g., engineers vs. operators).

5. **Confidence Measures for Recommendations**:
   - **Not explicitly defined** in the standard. However, confidence could be inferred from:
     - **Data Quality**: Validation of sensor inputs and **data transformation FEs**.
     - **Model Accuracy**: Alignment between **virtual models** in the DTE and the physical OME.
     - **Historical Validation**: Comparison of DTE predictions against past OME behavior (e.g., accuracy of maintenance forecasts).
   - The standard suggests that **confidence metrics** could be embedded in the **User Entity** to quantify the reliability of insights (e.g., probability scores for recommendations).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system described in **ISO 23247** adheres to a **comprehensive standardization framework**, primarily structured around **industry-specific and interoperability standards** to ensure consistency, interoperability, and compliance. Key elements include:

1. **Relevant Industry Standards**:
   - **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing**:
     - **Part 1**: Overview and general principles, establishing the **conceptual foundation** for DTs in manufacturing.
     - **Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**: Defines the **entity-based RA**, where:
       - **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., equipment, processes) are mapped to **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**.
       - **Functional Entities (FEs)** modularize DT functionality (e.g., data collection, user interaction), though implementation is not standardized.
       - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Standardizes the **interface** between physical OMEs and digital representations.
       - **User Entity**: Provides **interoperable interfaces** for human-DT interaction.
     - **Part 3**: Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, though not detailed in the provided documents.
     - **Part 4**: Information exchange standards for **data interoperability** between entities (specific protocols not mentioned).
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Implied as a **de facto standard** for **data exchange** between entities (e.g., sensors, DTEs, user interfaces), though not explicitly mandated by ISO 23247.

2. **Specifications Followed**:
   - **Entity-Based Modularity**: The RA enforces **standardized roles** for each entity (e.g., DTEs must represent OMEs accurately), but **technical specifications** (e.g., data formats, APIs) are not prescribed. This allows flexibility in **FE implementation** (e.g., using OPC UA, MQTT, or proprietary protocols).
   - **Data Interoperability**: ISO 23247 Part 4 outlines **information exchange requirements**, though specifics (e.g., **semantic models**, **data schemas**) are not provided. The standard implies adherence to **industry-wide data standards** (e.g., STEP, PLCopen) for **digital representation**.
   - **Security and Privacy**: Not detailed in the provided documents, but the **entity-based RA** suggests **role-based access control** and **data encryption** for sensitive OME information.

3. **Certification Requirements**:
   - **Not explicitly addressed** in the provided documents. However, compliance with **ISO 23247** could serve as a **de facto certification** for DT systems in manufacturing, ensuring alignment with **industry best practices**.
   - The standard does not specify **third-party certification bodies** or **audit processes**, but adherence to the RA could be verified through **conformance testing** (e.g., validating FE interoperability).

4. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **ISO 23247 Compliance**: The primary framework, ensuring:
     - **Functional Alignment**: DTEs must accurately reflect OMEs and support **real-time data exchange**.
     - **Lifecycle Governance**: The RA mandates **modular, entity-based development**, though lifecycle phases (e.g., deployment, retirement) are not standardized beyond the RA’s scope.
   - **Industry-Specific Frameworks**: The standard is tailored for **manufacturing**, with potential alignment to **automotive (e.g., McSCert Automotive Digital Twin)** or **process industries** standards (e.g., ISA-95 for control systems).

5. **Interoperability Standards Adopted**:
   - **OPC UA**: Implied as the **preferred protocol** for **device control and data collection**, enabling seamless integration between OMEs and DTEs.
   - **Information Exchange Standards (ISO 23247 Part 4)**: While not detailed, these standards likely include:
     - **Semantic Interoperability**: Common data models (e.g., **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) standards**) for OME representation.
     - **Syntactic Interoperability**: **Data formats** (e.g., JSON, XML) and **communication protocols** (e.g., REST, SOAP) for FE interactions.
   - **Modular Interoperability**: The **FE design** allows **plug-and-play compatibility** between different vendors’ components (e.g., a third-party analytics FE integrating with a DTE).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system described in **ISO 26262-1:2018** adheres to **ISO 26262 functional safety standards** for road vehicles, mandating rigorous cybersecurity and safety protocols. Key measures include:
- **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Compliance with **ISO 26262-8** (automotive cybersecurity) to mitigate threats via **secure communication channels**, **encryption (e.g., AES-128/256)**, and **secure boot processes** for embedded systems.
- **Access Control Mechanisms**: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with **multi-level authorization** (e.g., ASIL-decomposed permissions) to restrict data/modification access based on **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL A-D)**.
- **Authentication Systems**: Use of **cryptographic certificates** (e.g., X.509) and **hardware security modules (HSMs)** for device authentication, aligned with **ISO 26262-9** for secure communication in vehicle networks.
- **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: Implementation of **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** and **firewalls** to monitor and block unauthorized access, with **fault-tolerant designs** to prevent single points of failure.
- **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: **Redundancy and diversity** in critical components (e.g., dual-core processors, watchdog timers) to ensure graceful degradation under cyber-physical attacks, per **ISO 26262-5** requirements.
- **Risk Assessment Approaches**: Systematic **hazard analysis (FMEA/FMEDA)** and **cybersecurity risk assessments** (e.g., **STRIDE framework**) to identify vulnerabilities in the Digital Twin’s **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication layers**, as referenced in **3GPP TS 22.185** for adaptive cruise control systems.
- **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: Integration of **safety mechanisms** (e.g., **safety monitors**, **safety-critical data logging**) to ensure compliance with **ISO 26262-6** for system-level safety validation, with **time-triggered architectures (TTA)** for deterministic behavior in real-time scenarios.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
